summary:
This case meets classic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder due to repeated attacks involving both optic nerves and spinal cord, along with significant vision impairment that persists after steroid treatment—a hallmark feature distinguishing it from multiple sclerosis. Recurrent involvement patterns and incomplete visual recovery post-steroids support an NMOSD diagnosis over MS.

Final Answer: $\boxed{\text{Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder}}$

Okay, I need to summarize only what Agent1 reasoned about — focusing strictly on the key clinical points related to diagnosing this condition based on symptoms and responses observed.

Key facts include: Bilateral recurring optic neuritis plus transverse myelitis; markedly reduced visual acuity (especially right eye at 6/60); partial improvement (~50%) under corticosteroid therapy. These findings point toward NMOSD because such combinations occur more frequently there than in MS—and full or sustained visual recovery typically doesn't happen as well compared to MS cases even when treated early.

So keeping within two concise sentences focused solely on critical diagnostic clues:

Bilateral recurrent optic neuritis and transverse myelitis indicate strong suspicion for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder rather than multiple sclerosis given its distinct attack pattern. Persistent severe visual deficits despite moderate steroid responsiveness further differentiate NMOSD from MS clinically.
